submission.title: What study score can I genuinely expect with these marks?
submission.body:
Methods: 46, 40, (1 sac left)
English: 60, 75, 80, 76 (1 sac left to do and haven’t received marks for another sac)
Psychology: 80, 62, 92
Legal Studies (my worst subject): 62, 52
HHD: 96, 96, 90 (1 sac left to do and haven’t received marks for another sac)
Business study score from last year: 32

comments.body: What scores did U get for business
✨✨*nobody can answer this because it depends on the strength of your school and cohort*✨✨

Methods: 25-30
English: 30-34
Psychology: 31-34
Legal: 22-25
HHD: 31-35

My basis for all of these? Just made it all up, without knowing how hard your sacs are and what rank you are no one can make a good guess.
Raw?

What if I had a really bad school? Like it’s a public school and we’re notorious for not performing well and for approximately all my subjects the cohort is getting around the same marks except for some high achievers in each subject?

its still extremely hard to say, you could try a study-score calculator online, even then it probably wouldnt be very accurate. 

but If i was you i'd be more worried about getting that raw 25 on methods assuming you need it as a prereq because 46 40 in a weak cohort wont cut it atm
I got around 50-60 with one 80 once and got a B+ on the exam
I only have the probability SAC left and honestly I don’t know if I’m cut out for it. Do you have any tips if you’ve done methods before? Any resources?
Omg even for HHD? I was relying on that study score cuz it’s my best subject rn…

if im not mistaken methods has a 33-66% sac to exam split towards your ss, if you're able to extremely well on your sac and especially exam you still have a chance.

For methods its all about application, you want to learn the content as fast as possible and pump out as many textbook/past sac/checkpoint questions as you can. their should be no shortage of practice questions as long as you have a textbook. familiarise yourself with every possible question and focus on questions youve made mistakes on in the past.
I did prob yesterday, I think you need to get really proficient in using your calculator (ours was all tech active) cause so much of that unit relies on your CAS skills
Do U remember the grade U got for the units or nah cause Ur 50 might've been bumped up cause I'm wondering I'm averaging 79 for u3 and probably like rank 5 if I I can get ss of 40
its pretty sarcastic, its highly unlikely u get 31 when u average 90+
yep and gotta understand which to use for different types of questions - classic importance of interpretation of questions
LMAO IM SO SORRY I CANT read social cues if my life depended on it 😓
Alright thank you so much
Will do 🫡 thank you

if u maintain ur grades and get 90 or more for the exam, you are probably looking at mid or high 40's, its hard to predict cause ur entire cohort could be getting 80-100 making ur 90 basically just a bit above average although thats unlikely to be the case
That’s the one thing I suck at honestly, I can never determine what the question is asking 😭
I think I got a B for one unit? I’ll check for you once I get home
I can tell you what grades my friend got cuz she got a 44 study score for business :)
Ok sure, its probably all A+'s though im guessin
if u got B, its probably that ur score got bumped up
I think it might be possible cuz my teachers marking was extremely strict and the exam for last year was really easy so I performed better
yeah fair
totally like if i knew what they wanted maybe i wouldn’t struggle as much HAHAHA

I was being sarcastic (and very conservative) with my guesses, but tbh they likely will get 35-40 (remember only like 8% of people get above a 40, including selective schools and geniuses). Which with scaling will likely mean less then 40.
Tbh I hope the best, I hope they get a 45ss, but we honestly can’t predict it

thats 1017 people who get above 40 in hhd last year, if they get 90 for the exam thats an mid - high A+ which reinforces their score for their sacs to be ''fair'' so they should get 40+ at that rate but there are many other facotrs however 90% on the exam should gurantee a 40+ pretty much
I predict no higher than a 39 ATAR

Worked on many different ATAR calculators:
• Methods: 22-24
• English: 27-29
• Psych: 31-34
• Legal: 26-28
• HHD: 35-36
• Business: 32

He won't get 40 for the exam if his cohort is a public school. 

My school was also bad and kids regularly averaged 90's on SACS and got 35-36 maximum.

I highly doubt it. 

I had friends who got 100% on most business SACs and only got only just over 40.

Most schools don't teach it well.
Every public school has people getting 40s

Yeah, the ones who do that ace the exam. 

There's a very good chance this bloke won't ace the exam, I'm being realistic.
Basically hating now

Am I? 

I went to a notoriously bad school. We had nearly 3,000 students and an average study score of 26.

We had VERY few get over 40 with those marks. You MAY if you get 100% on the exam, but realistically speaking you will NOT be.
Pass?
hhd is gonna be way higher than 35-36, the A+ in the exam is a high 60, a lot of people don’t get perfect SACs but still mog on the exam with an 80+ and end up with a 44-50, just to give OP hope
Thsi is such weird scaling tho? Like how does it make sense to consistently get the best you can and still not even push the 40’s
Holy shit thsi is much lower than I would’ve expected

Don't give false hope. 

Accept low fate and if high fate goes your way be grateful.
what’s the false hope bro? 96,96, 90 are really high sac scores
Because the SACs are only 50% of the final mark..?

And they are only 50% of the final mark. 

Judging by their other subjects, they will NOT replicate those on the exam.
bro really blocked me, yeah ok ur just better than them and they won’t do well on the exam…
he’s just being egotistical, if u work hard like i said you definitely won’t struggle to get a 43+
TYSM for your kind words and hope, I was really about to cry over that guys comments 😭😭

just don’t start cruising yeah? if you want a good study score just keep doing what ur doing now and then do more for exams. Even if you do bad on a few days it’s fine because the exam is so hard (let’s u have a higher chance to redeem yourself). You’re doing great now you should be proud of yourself

submission.title: Is the travel time really that bad for Monash? Esp for anyone doing Bachelor of Laws
submission.body:
Hi, I’m a prospective student (still in year 12 rn) looking to get into Monash Bachelor of Law (Honours). 
The biggest thing holding me back from putting it as my first preference is the travel time, from my house it would take about 2:45 hours to go to Monash uni via public transport. I wanted to ask any students that were currently undertaking Bachelor of Law how often do you go to uni? How many days a week approximately? If anyone had a similar problem as me how did you overcome it? 
Any advice is appreciated (even if it doesn’t relate to my questions, if you have any application tips please comment those as well). Ty

comments.body: I would consider another uni, almost 3 hours of travel time is very long.

Takes me about an hour to get to uni by public transport. If you are set on doing law at Monash, I would recommend minimising the days you have to show up at uni, and/or driving to uni.

I'm currently on uni one day a week, so even though that one day is very long, I can minimise the time wasted on public transport. However, since it takes you nearly 3 hours to get to uni, I wouldn't recommend it because you would have to wake up at ungodly hours and get home super late - definitely drive/live on res if you can, or don't go to monash :(

No application tips, unfortunately your atar will speak for itself in vtac
Law is rlly hard to get all ur classes on 1 day - expect 2-3 days of classes. 2:45 is mad btw.

being so honest with u here as a first year law student … idk about future years, but the actual classes themselves are a big waste of time. all information is online, and if you get a good lecturer /tutor then you might have some extra gain but otherwise i’ve learned everything exclusively at home and questioned myself everyday why i’ve gone to uni 🤷‍♀️ however contact hours are about 5-6 a week, can be squeezed into two days.

Hi there!
Am in a similar situation to what you described. I am currently studying Law and Commerce (first year) and have a commute of 2.5 hours (each way). I wish I could say that I’m loving uni, but I can’t, and that’s all because of the commute. You can’t join clubs as they are usually held on campus (or in neighbouring suburbs like Dandenong) from 6:30-9:30, which makes club participation not tangible. Additionally, hanging out with fellow peers is impossible as you ultimately live in the middle of nowhere, meaning that you can’t really create any lasting friendships and that after one semester, you likely won’t ever see them again. Note: when I refer to these situations as impossible, they’re obviously not, just not practical at all.

As for the workload, it’s definitely not easy. The readings (especially for first semester) was pretty overwhelming, but it’s definitely doable if you put in the work. I have tons of extra curricular commitments but I still managed to get a 75 WAM first semester with no legal knowledge at all.

What I would also like to mention that I think is understated a lot is the Monash law culture. I found that 90% of the students are super rich, like having come out of Caulfield grammar,haileybury etc, and as a dude who graduated from a dodgy local public school, I found it rather different to the general demographics of my former peers. Nevertheless, all the peers are still very friendly (some are toxic and fake asf tho, but then again, they’re usually the smartest too). As for the faculty, they are AMAZING (If you do do law next year, pick CLAIRE for FOL, she’s the best lecturer I’ve had so far, and ANTONY for crim law, his also great).

I would say the only good thing that comes out of the commute is the ability to see the sunrise and sunset. I don’t do any studying on the train/bus as it’s just not practical, but rather just listen to music and watch the sun. 

ALSO PLEASE TAKE THIS FROM ME, you don’t need breaks between classes, I made this grave mistake and ended going 3 days a week last semester (it was awful). Now I only go one, but I go from 8-4 nonstop (yes the 5 AM wake up makes me wanna cry every week), but that’s all for the week (I do wag 2 classes on Wednesday though ;) )!

So, to answer your question, yes the commute is lowkey unbearable, but it really depends on your own aspirations and reasons. The only reason I’m at Monash is cause they have the best LLB course in Victoria, and I’m relatively committed to becoming a lawyer. Nevertheless, looking in the bright side,this experience will allow you to gain resilience and determination which others may not get to experience.

Hopefully my lengthy insights are of help, feel free to DM me if you have any further concerns or questions :)) BEST OF LUCK FOR YOUR VCE EXAMS!

2 hours 45 minutes is crazy. I’m 1 hour 45 and even that is a lot. It does take a toll on you eventually and trust me, you will be more likely to skip classes using the excuse of “it’s too far away I can’t be bothered” 

Let’s say you have a 8:00am class, are you really going to leave your house at 5:15am to go? That means waking up at 4:00am or 4:30am…. Maybe you will do it on the first day but then you will never attend that class again. Let’s say you have a class from 4-6:30pm… are you really going to go if it means you get home at 9:15pm or later? 

Especially with Law, you most likely if not definitely, won’t get all your classes in one day meaning you will be making the commute multiple times a week. I don’t think any university is worth spending 5 hours+ commuting to.
Unless you’re willing to move to the Clayton area or live on campus, I would find a university closer to your home.
Once you hit second year units you actually get graded on tutorial participation and attendance rip

From a 4th year law/comm student with an 1.25 hour commute each way - I think law is doable especially from second year onwards as all the important lectures/seminars are recorded. The only classes you HAVE to come in for are participation based tutorials. 

If you are able to do the readings, watch seminar recordings, and get legal experience locally, you can do law at Monash. 

If you are dead set on doing law and get the marks for Monash, don’t comprise on university. Going to a G08 uni for law will open more career options for you. 

Commerce is another issue tho - cause so many classes are participation based.

not sure if it's achievable but at that point it'd be better off that you sharehouse closer or live on res. but given the renting crisis and how high prices are fair if you can't.

I have to say that probably most classes will have recordings if you don't attend but it really helps to go to workshops and tutorials. especially some units tutorials attendance is examined so you have no choice but to go.

the workload depends on your capacity. I've heard some people on reddit say that doing 3 or 4 law units is fully manageable. youd have a 1 hr tutorial and 3hr workshop per unit. And youd have to do alot of reading at home, making notes and structures to help you do tutorial questions. but that's if you want to get a high wam, which is not everyones goal. so don't feel like you have to do everything if it's too much.

But I personally couldn't manage 3 or 4 law units a semester. Cuz it meant having 6-8 classes a week on top of working, volunteering and social commitments. 2 law units and 2 other units was more manageable for me and probably most people. Another thing is there's usually a 30-40% assignment for each unit which can be a class test, research assignment or group assignment. And all law units have a 2 hr and 30 min exam. so if you take 3 or 4 law units it's going to be like mini VCE every semester unless you don't care about your WAM at all. but again why i prefer taking 2 law units and 2 non law like arts. cuz you could just do arts units without a hard final assessment and then you can focus better on your law units.

but not everyone has that luxury or some ppl can't tolerate staying 5 years to do their degree. I'm doing a double degree. some people through pathways or other could only do bachelor of law honours single degree.

honestly commute has not been a problem for me, but I doubt I would be able to do half I do now if it took me that long to commute. it'd be super hard to do anything on campus. 

hope you figure something out and while Monash law is probably the best place to do law,  don't close your mind to other closer options. Or the possibility that you can move closer if you have the money.
It’s can be worth approaching your tutor about participation marks if you have barriers - some will be understanding. AFAIK there are no marks for attendance in law, only participation.
yes also wanted to say good luck learning corporation law without going to seminars, its so dense.

Unfortunately attendance is considered as part of your participation. You are usually allowed to miss one tutorial without getting penalised but the remainder are graded unless you receive special consideration
Ugh I haven’t hit corps yet but I have heard nightmare stories

Wow!! Thank you sooo much for thsi detailed of a response. This was definitely helpful, I have the same reason for wanting to go to Monash as you, it simply has the best LLB course in Victoria. I did have a question on the college community tho, you said it was mostly rich kids from those bougie schools, as a public school student myself, how did you go about making acquaintances or friends?

I wish it was that easy but unfortunately law courses are really limited in the small amount of unis that do offer them. Monash is by far the best one offering that course and so automatically I’m aspiring to join them but we’ll see where my atar takes me.
Oh wow so does that mean ur attendance counts as part of your grades? Sorry I’m not really familiar with the uni language 😅
Aw man this seems more impossible by the minute 😔
Do you have any experience in looking for accomodation around Clayton? Or does Monash offer accomodation? Honestly I’ve been reading a lot of these replies and it’s really making me question Monash…
What do you mean by participation based? I’m not really familiar with the uni lingo 😅
IK and 2:45 is the least amount of time 😭if I miss even one bus or tram it goes up to like 3 hours and 10-20 minutes

Hey this is really interesting! What double degree are you doing? Does doing a double degree decrease the amount of work for your law degree? I was under the impression that you have to do double the work in even less time. Sorry if that’s sounds dumb my school doesn’t have the best pathways team so I haven’t been taught a lot of this stuff and it’s really hard to research into it.

I personally don’t because moving out of home for uni was never really something I considered. My commute is long but I can handle it and I drive most of the time so it’s about an hours drive which isn’t too bad. 

If you’re going to move out in order to attend Monash then I recommend student accomodation over finding a place in Clayton independently. With this though, you have to consider whether it’s possible financially as for both student accomodation or the renting of a house/apartment in Clayton will set you back 25-30 or even 40k a year. If you and your family are financially prepared for that then you can get in contact with Monash and they will assist you, or you can start looking for a place to rent. Either way, whether you chose accomodation or the commute (which I strongly advise against) you have a lot to consider. 

Me personally, if I were you I would not chose Monash. There’s there University of Melbourne which you might be closer to or literally anything else. Like I said, I’m 1 hour 45 mins away but if Unimelb had the course that I wanted, I would have 10000000% gone there over Monash as unimelb is only 35 mins away from me via public transport and driving
😨😨😨😨😨, you do realise you will be spending almost an entire school day just travelling to and from uni right? Say goodbye to your social life or the idea of joining any clubs

Uni of melb was always my first preference however they don’t offer any undergraduate law courses and those are only ones I can get into. They only offer Juris Doctor and obviously I can’t do that right now. The thing with other unis is that they can’t compete with Monash when it comes to job prospects or career pathways (as I have been told, correct me if I’m wrong) and also the unis are pretty limited in VIC. There’s only 3 other unis that I could have even applied to.

In the ordinary course of things, attendance is a precondition for participation marks. It attracts no marks whatsoever itself. I’m suggesting that students might find some flexibility if they’re open about their circumstances. 

And yes, generally can miss one, although IME the threshold for SC for missed tutes is low and the alternative assessment provided pretty straightforward.

doing a double degree doesnt decrease the amount of work you do overall for law, but it reduces the law specific workload you do in a single semester.

for eg, if you do commerce and law, a semester at full time load would contain 2 law units and 2 commerce units. meanwhile, a single law degree fulltime would contain 4 law units in one semester from year 2 onwards.   
  
 people prefer the 2 law units (me included) because 

- law is pretty heavy in terms of content knowledge; people can avoid doing 4 law units of heavy workload and worry only about 2 law units in one single semester and;

- when you get bored/exhausted of studying law, you can always switch learning content completely 

a double degree is longer, but so much more worth it.
Like you have to rock up to class and participate to get the mark.

OP at the end of the day doesn't matter what uni you come from. But how you make of the uni experience and work ethic. Everyone is like I want to go to XXX because of reputation. But no one ever considers ever the commute to get there. Spending almost 3 hrs to get there is too much. You'll start hating uni, trust me. You can use that time for sometime else. Imagine having an 8:30 class? Now add 3 hours commute and waking up and getting ready beforehand. By the time you get there you will be so grumpy and out of it you won't even be paying attention because you're so tired. There's plenty of unis that offer this degree. 

If you really want to go to monash, maybe try getting a your license or car. It might be a bit bearable.

You’re welcome! The “rich kid” part was mostly targeted towards law students. I’ve had commerce and IT classes and they consisted of a wider range of people. 

As for making friends with law students, I found it pretty easy. At first it’s super intimidating, but as long as you’re nice, you’ll fit in. You’ll find that after a few classes, everyone has their own “grouped table”. What I did have to do was remove my “public school slang”, as I live in a predominantly middle eastern suburb, so there’s that. Aside from that, I’d also definitely encourage joining the Monash LSS if you decide on Monash law, they are pretty amazing.
I reckon just do your degree in 20 years when suburban rail loop is finished that’ll fix it /s

try moving closer, I'm a law student and I also work simultaneously. I can afford my rent of approximately 850/month with 20 hours of working per week. I lived in Oakleigh, before driving, I take the bus which the bus loop is right next to the two buildings I go to for my classes so it's not that big of a deal (the 2.5 hours part is tho)

While it is true that going to a GO8 uni (Monash or Melbourne) for law does give you an edge, at the end of the day it comes down to how you perform and the type of candidate you are. You could be 10x more capable than uni Melb or Monash graduates and it will show. You may have to work harder coming from a job go8 uni to compete with the others but it doesn’t mean you will be unemployed because you didn’t go to Monash.
Find someone local who will let you stay at theirs one night per week

if rmit is closer, i would say just go there. i have abt 1:45 commute and though I only go twice a week, a find myself skipping class a lot bc its so far away and I rather use that time to do other work. deadly excuse esp during exams bc I find myself missing a lot of classes
Do you see significant impacts on your grades due to the travel time? Like would you say law is a very participation and attendance required type of degree, or can you self learn the concepts?

You can definitely self learn but youll hear this time and time again that law is all abt networking. Making those connections in class and asking questions in class is always going to give you a better experience. In terms of lectures/seminars, watching them online is probably more better bc the lecturers talk soooo slow

No worries! Yes sort of. 10% of your grade is based on your participation in classes. While it is overall participation, they’ll usually require you to show up to all of them except one to be graded properly.

I’m currently doing the course, my travel time is around 1.30hr. At first it was annoying, but I got used to it pretty quick. Have you thought about getting a car (not the most easy option but saves a lot of travel time and gives you more freedom) or perhaps living on campus (is about 300 a week, if you can find a job near or on campus it’ll honestly be a good option).

submission.title: What does a Bachelor of art look like?
submission.body:
As the title suggests, I’m not trying to get into a bachelor of art but instead opting to do a double degree with a bachelor of law. My only question is what does the workload look like? I don’t want to underestimate the subject and assume it’s going to be easier than Law however from what I’ve heard it’s a relatively easier uni course. 
So if you are currently doing anything in relation to Bachelor of Law or know anything about its course structure, assignments, and exams pls drop it down below! All help is appreciated!

comments.body: Shit I meant “if you are currently doing anything in relation to a bachelor of ART”

The Bachelor of Arts is a generalist degree, but you can pursue a major in a diverse discipline such as: economics, ancient history, international relations, politics, criminology, human rights, and many more. You can have a look at the course structure and double degree combinations for the Bachelor of Arts here: [A2000 Monash](https://handbook.monash.edu/2023/courses/A2000). Arts units usually do not have exams (although some actually do in the form of online tests) so this makes it relatively easier to pass compared to Law units. If you're interested in a double degree with Law, it might take 5 years to finish both.

im not doing arts, rather global studies/law but a lot of the electives overlap. its definitely easier compared to law and I find that its beneficial bc I don't have to do 4 law units at a time bc that gets really hectic really quick. if you do art/law you most likely will do 2 law units per semester and 2 art units and art units are usually pretty chill ngl. i would definitely say try the double degree and if you really don't like it then drop it but more likely you'll find the arts units more entertaining than the law units because they are dry af
Tysm!! I'll be sure to look through this!

Hmm, thats what I've been hearing from a majority of people and as someone who would prefer a more creative focus I reckon a bachelor of arts as a double degree might work out. You mentioned that you can drop out of the art degree, I have a question regarding that can I exclusively drop the art degree and continue with my law one? and if so can I maybe change degrees like a semester in (for example change from double degree law/art to law/global studies)?

I did a double degree in arts/business specialist. Art major was Japanese and minor was behavioural studies. Business specialist was accounting. Took me 4 years to complete but totally worth it as I loved learning Japanese and it was a nice way to balance hobby with studying :)
Wow that’s so cool! I’ll look into it further because this sounds exactly like something I’d want to do

You can exclusively drop out of the art one and continue with law, you just have to make sure that if you consider the withdrawal and census dates to make sure it doesnt show up on your transcript. But you can always contact Monash Connect if u get to that stage. For the latter, im not quite sure but there probably is a way. Monash Connect would be the go to place for consulting abt that. They r genuinely really helpful

You can exclusively drop out of the art one and continue with law, you just have to make sure that if you consider the withdrawal and census dates to make sure it . show up on your transcript. But you can always contact Monash Connect if u get to that stage. For the latter, im not quite sure but there probably is a way. Monash Connect would be the go to place for consulting abt that. They r genuinely really helpful
Tysm!! I’ll be sure to look into it

submission.title: transport
submission.body:
Got Monash for lots of my course preferences, but the problem is it’s so fkn far! I live in Essendon, my family is likely moving possible Epping or even Craigeburn but that would be a long ride . Does anybody travel from these locations or anywhere around them? How difficult is it? how do you get there? Did you end up renting a room/place closer to Monash? 

Thanks

comments.body:
Depending on your course, some units might offer online classes which you can attend remotely. And you can cram your in-person classes 2-3 days a week. 

But if you’re required to attend all 5 days in-person, it might be worth renting a sharehouse nearer Monash

Hey! I live in Epping/Cragieburn and am in my second year at Monash.

I won’t lie- the commute is definitely long (2.5 hours commute just to get to uni taking buses/trains 🥲) and very tiring. However, if you are able to fit all of your classes into 2-3 days it is manageable. I’ve considered renting near campus (but probably can’t manage it financially at the moment) and if that’s an option for you, I’d strongly recommend! It would make life much easier :) 

Also- I sometimes drive (takes me about 1.5 hours to get to uni during peak traffic) but I find this personally worse than taking public transport as it’s even more tiring tbh (having to stay constantly focused etc.)
tysm!!!
Hey, I have legit the same problem as you. I live in craigieburn, however I am looking for flat mates to bunk nearby Monash. Lemme know if ur interested!

travelling from north suburbs as well, trust me its not as bad as you think. it might feel hard at first but you get used to it. the craigieburn line takes like 45 mins from craigie to the city and then from the city to huntingdale there is almost always an express service running on the pakenham/cranbourn/westall lines which skips stations and takes like 25 odd minutes. so it would be like 1.5 to get to uni but if you are watching lectures or doing readings that time flies. the only downside is that sometimes you can use the excuse that uni is too far to skip a lot of classes and if participating in any clubs gets a bit tough because they have all of their events at like 5-6pm and it can carry on late at night. if you are set on monash, go for it, but if you can afford some flexibility, go for a uni in the city.
Btw Anything to do around the Uni?

submission.title: How important are trial exams?
submission.body:
Sooo like a few other students I've been completely burnt out this term break and have therefore literally done zero work for like a week (except making cue cards and making my methods summary notes). My trial exams begin in a weeks time and I have all 5 subjects back to back.  
As I have not been studying quite intensely I don't expect high marks on my trial exam however, I want to know whether my marks from any trial exams go to VCAA and would play a role in determining the letter grade for my final exam?

comments.body: Nope, there’s no marks involved. Just try your best and see what gaps you have.

Nah doesn’t contribute to anything. But use your trial exam marks as a learning experience and take on the feedback given by assessor’s to see what areas you could further work on during exam revision

I found them usefull in getting me farmiliar with exams. once your done, and you get a taste, it was encouraging to keep studying. plus, i reccon its more comfortable if you already did an exam under exam conditions. Also, its good practice, and you can test your notes and shit in a good environment. its just good all around!
Doesn’t contribute to ur actual exam, however, for my school in some of the subjects like math it is going to be used for ranking, especially between those who have similar ranking
LMAO MAKING CUE CARDS LIKE BENEDICT CHUR
alrightio tysmm, also I notice that you got a 30 raw in methods... that is the study score that I am aiming for what letter grades did you get to be able to achieve that?

Different schools have different Letters, so ill give you my marks based on each sac:

SAC 2: Modelling / Problem Solving 1 - 70%  
SAC 3: Modelling / Problem Solving Task 2 - 65%  
SAC 1 Part A and B: 52%

overall, i roughly got a D+ Semester 1, and for semester 2, it doesent say on my class page for some reason, but i estimate between D+ and C This wouldve gotten me a study score below 30.  
However, i got a B in the Exam, witch braught me up to a raw 30.

to tell you the truth, it doesent matter what you get in class. if you bomb the year, but fucking ace the exam. you can get a pretty good score. and also, to get more complicated, your results for sacs depend on how well your class, school and year level did, idk i dont really get it.

My advice, do as many practice exams as you can, with a reference book. make sure you have a reference book too.  
Literaly thats all i did for weeks, practice exams.
ohh how do they use it for ranking??

Tysm!! I do have one more question, about your reference book this time. How detailed was your reference book? I've looked through some other reddit threads and a lot of people are encouraging that you don't bring a reference book in or don't waste time on making one. How much did your references book help you in your exams?
like if there’s two individuals tied for the same ranking they use that to determine who goes on top
ohh i see - thanks!

submission.title: Does anyone do a politics, philosophy, and economics bachelor degree?
submission.body: My question is as the title suggests. I'm looking to do a Monash bachelor of Law with a double degree in politics, philosophy, and economics. However, my only problem is that I am not a business keen person (I hated business in school and just can't imagine myself in any business heavy courses) so does the politics, philosophy, and economics degree have a heavy business influence? Also how is the difficulty of the degree as a whole? As i'm already doing Law I don't want another degree thats going to be just as content heavy. tyyy

comments.body: Not really business heavy at all. If you choose econ as your specialisation you have a fair bit of flexibility with the units you choose and can tailor it more towards public economics. If you choose politics or philosophy as your specialisation you’ll only have to do intro to micro and macroeconomics which are fairly simple units. The degree is pretty generalist so it’s not overly content heavy.
Amazing tysm!

i’m doing this degree now (law/ppe). I would say that the ppe side is definitely easier, and depending on what you specialise in, you only have to do 2 introductory units for the other disciplines you’re not specialising in, so you can decide more or less. econ doesn’t really have a business focus unless you want it to
What are you specialising in?
Politics

submission.title: Do you have to do practice exams for revision?
submission.body: I'm seeing everyone else doing lots of practice exams on this subreddit. And I'm starting to feel kind of bad even though I'm still doing lots of revision. I don't know it feels like I'm not doing enough. So should I be doing full practice exams in timed conditions? Or are singular practice questions from practice exams enough?

comments.body: If you find that you struggle with timing then definitely do full timed exams. The whole point of closed book closed time exams is to stimulate exam conditions so it’s less uncomfortable when it actually gets to exam period. I would recommend you do a few exams for each subject atleast but at the end of the day it’s up to how you learn.
Ok thank you :)!
